confidence.disclosure: Reconstructed from al-Tabari's annalistic Tarikh and al-Ya'qubi. The four major phases — the civil war with al-Amin from Merv, the long residence in Merv, the slow journey to Baghdad via Tus and Nahrawan, and the final Byzantine campaigns ending at Tarsus in 833 — are all well attested. Some intermediate stops during the Byzantine wars rely on al-Tabari's year-by-year summaries rather than day-by-day records.

sources: Hugh Kennedy, The Early Abbasid Caliphate (1981)

journey.event: Born in Baghdad on the night his father Harun al-Rashid acceded to the caliphate.
Accompanied Harun al-Rashid to Mecca, where the famous succession document dividing the empire between him and al-Amin was deposited in the Ka'ba.
Sent east as viceroy of Khurasan with his headquarters at Merv.
His general Tahir ibn al-Husayn defeated al-Amin's army under Ali ibn Isa at the Battle of Rayy, opening the civil war in his favour.
Proclaimed caliph in Merv after Tahir's forces captured Baghdad and killed al-Amin.
Designated the eighth Shia Imam Ali al-Rida as his successor in Merv, a controversial decision that alienated the Abbasid elite in Baghdad.
Travelled through Tus, where Ali al-Rida died (reputedly by poisoning) and was buried; al-Ma'mun prayed at his father Harun al-Rashid's tomb there.
Continued westward through Nishapur on the long return journey to Baghdad.
Arrived at Nahrawan near Baghdad in August 819 after a journey of nearly two years from Merv.
Entered Baghdad in state and took up residence as caliph, ending the long Khurasani absence.
Launched the first of his personal Byzantine campaigns, establishing Tarsus as the forward base.
Led a second summer campaign deep into Byzantine Cappadocia.
Travelled personally to Egypt to suppress the Bashmurite revolt in the Nile Delta.
Held court in Fustat and is said to have visited the Pyramids of Giza.
Fell ill at Podandos on the Byzantine frontier during his third campaign and died on 7 August 833.
